original_abstract: Reasoning is a fundamental aspect of human intelligence that plays a crucial role in activities such as problem solving, decision making, and critical thinking. In recent years, large language models (LLMs) have made significant progress in natural language processing, and there is observation that these models may exhibit reasoning abilities when they are sufficiently large. However, it is not yet clear to what extent LLMs are capable of reasoning. This paper provides a comprehensive overview of the current state of knowledge on reasoning in LLMs, including techniques for improving and eliciting reasoning in these models, methods and benchmarks for evaluating reasoning abilities, findings and implications of previous research in this field, and suggestions on future directions. Our aim is to provide a detailed and up-to-date review of this topic and stimulate meaningful discussion and future work.
humanized_abstract: Reasoning is a key capacity of the human mind and essential to many cognitive tasks such as problem solving, decision making and thinking critically. Recently, large language models (LLMs) have made impressive strides in the field of natural language processing (NLP) and there is growing consensus that larger models can often reason when they are capable of carrying out the task in the first place. However, the extent to which an LLM is able to reason is still a matter of debate. This paper presents a full review of the state of the art in respect of LLMs and reasoning, including ways of improving and eliciting reasoning, as well as the methods and benchmarks for assessing the reasoning capacity of LLMs. We summarize key findings and implications of existing work on this topic, and provide a number of recommendations for future work on this topic with the goal of providing a comprehensive and timely review that will serve to further discussion and activity in this field.
